summary: Gruber uses the New York Times's first-ever misprinted crossword grid — in 84 years of publishing — as a springboard to articulate the difference between 'hardware brain' and 'software brain.' He argues that print's permanence creates a discipline and focus that digital lacks, since mistakes in print are forever while software mistakes can be patched. He connects this to Apple's incoming CEO John Ternus, suggesting that Ternus's hardware background will instill a perfectionist, slow-down-and-get-it-right ethos at Apple. This, Gruber argues, would mean Apple doubling down on its own path while the rest of the industry races frantically through the AI hype cycle.
key_points: The NYT printed the wrong crossword grid for the first time in 84 years, and solvers blamed themselves rather than the paper — a testament to the trust print institutions carry.
Print is effectively hardware: permanent, unforgivable of mistakes, and demanding of focus. The web is software: patchable, forgiving, and encouraging speed over perfection.
Hardware brain says slow down and strive for perfection; software brain says go faster and fix mistakes later.
Gruber draws a direct line from the hardware-vs-software-brain distinction to Apple's CEO transition, suggesting John Ternus's hardware background will shape Apple's direction.
Apple zigging while the AI-obsessed industry zags feels right to Gruber — a validation of discipline over frenzy.
money_quotes: Print, effectively, is hardware. Atoms, not bits. The web is literally software.
If you make a mistake in a CPU such that it results in incorrect floating-point math, perhaps only in 1 out of every 9 billion calculations, people will remember the mistake 30 years later.
Hardware brain says Slow down; do less; focus; strive for perfection and never settle for less than excellence; mistakes are forever.
Software brain says Go faster; do more; the only mistake you can't fix is having gone too slow.
But by printing the wrong grid in the Sunday magazine last week, they made a mistake that some people will never forget (and some will never forgive).
Going to press is stressful but exhilarating. There's an adrenaline rush that comes with giving the go-ahead to start a very expensive large-scale full-color press run. The stress focuses the mind.

If his background in hardware means that incoming Apple CEO John Ternus has hardware brain, and will lead Apple accordingly, that suggests Apple will double down on zigging in the midst of a still-escalating AI hype cycle that has the rest of the industry zagging ever more frenetically.
key_insight: The permanence of hardware — whether printed crosswords or physical products — demands a discipline of perfection that software culture has abandoned, and Apple under John Ternus may embrace that hardware mindset as its strategic advantage.
